quote: even under these minimal conditions, model performance degrades as input length increases, often in surprising and non-uniform ways.
as needle-question similarity decreases, model performance degrades more significantly with increasing input length.

performance is often highest when relevant information occurs at the beginning or end of the input context, and significantly degrades when models must access relevant information in the middle of long contexts.
all the top open- and closed-weight LLMs we test exhibit significantly lower performance in multi-turn conversations than single-turn, with an average drop of 39% across six generation tasks.
when LLMs take a wrong turn in multi-turn conversation, they get lost and do not recover; degradation decomposes into a minor loss in aptitude and a significant increase in unreliability.
maximization-based decoding methods such as beam search lead to degeneration: output text that is bland, incoherent, or gets stuck in repetitive loops.
good context engineering means finding the smallest possible set of high-signal tokens that maximize the likelihood of some desired outcome.
Context Poisoning, Context Distraction, Context Confusion, and Context Clash are four ways longer contexts cause agents to fail.

While all models claim context size of 32k tokens or greater, only half of them can effectively handle sequence length of 32K; almost all models fall below the threshold before reaching the claimed context lengths.
At 32K, 11 models drop below 50% of their strong short-length baselines; even GPT-4o falls from 99.3% to 69.7%.

the per-step error rate itself rises as the task progresses; conditioning models on their own error-prone history increases the likelihood of future errors (self-conditioning), and this is not mitigated by scaling model size.
the decision advantage of autoregressive reasoning trajectories decays exponentially with execution length, motivating structural segmentation and intermediate stabilization.
keeping the KV of initial tokens (attention sinks) largely recovers the performance of window attention; just 4 initial tokens suffice to stabilize attention over up to 4 million tokens.
retrieval reordering prioritizes documents with higher scores at the beginning and end of the input sequences to mitigate the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon.
marginal returns diminish substantially at higher budgets and models exhibit overthinking, where extended reasoning is associated with abandoning previously correct answers.
the 1.5B model loses 2.4% accuracy when increasing from 512 to 1024 tokens; beyond saturation points additional tokens can reduce accuracy.
As conversations get longer, models struggle to maintain focus across the full history. Compaction keeps the active context focused and performant by replacing stale content with concise summaries.
